dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a diabetic problem at a residence.
Hi, I'm Alex. What made you call 911 today?
She's weak, confused, and not herself-high blood sugar is the likely cause.
She is alert, oriented to person and place, but appears lethargic and less verbal.
Pulse is 81, respirations 33, blood pressure 174/64, SpO2 91. Blood glucose is not available.
Placing you on supplemental 2 L/min oxygen via nasal cannula to support SpO2.
Skin is pink, warm, and dry with poor turgor. No bleeding or pallor.
First IV attempt failed, but a 22-gauge IV was successfully established in the right forearm.
Administered 250 mL of normal saline via IV to support hydration.
Started 4-lead ECG monitoring to assess heart rhythm for baseline.
A 12-lead EKG shows sinus rhythm with minimal evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy and ST-T wave abnormalities.
Blood sugar has been consistently high since dialysis on Wednesday. She's been lethargic and less verbal since then.
SpO2 is now 95%. The patient reports no pain at the moment.
Vitals: Pulse 95, respirations 30, BP 196/90, SpO2 95, EKG sinus rhythm.
Last vitals: Pulse 93, respirations 26, BP 193/82, SpO2 96, EKG shows stable sinus rhythm.
We will transport to [COMPANY_NAME] as requested by the patient's daughter.
Patient is being carried via a two-person lift to the stretcher and secured for transport.
We're at the hospital now. The patient is stable and secured.
Transferring the patient from the stretcher to the hospital bed using a sheet pull.
Nurses, this is a 65-year-old female diabetic with no reported pain, poor skin turgor, and altered mental status.
Care has been transferred to an RN in ED Room 14 with no incidents reported.
You mention no pain, and our records show no pain reported at any point.
